content.text: Text embedding models convert strings into fixed-length vectors that capture semantic meaning. The model family you choose — sparse lexical, dense bi-encoder, or cross-encoder — determines the accuracy-speed tradeoff and how you build your index. Understanding the families helps you pick the right tool for each stage of a retrieval pipeline.

attrs.alt: Three text embedding families: sparse (TF-IDF/BM25), dense bi-encoder, and cross-encoder reranker — with trade-offs and best-use cases.

content.text: Sparse models produce vocabulary-sized vectors where most values are zero. The non-zero entries correspond to words present in the text. BM25 is the canonical example — fast, no training required, and excellent for exact keyword matching.

from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi

docs = [
    "cats are small carnivores that hunt rodents",
    "dogs are loyal companions and working animals",
    "Python is a high-level programming language",
]
tokenized = [d.split() for d in docs]
bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenized)

scores = bm25.get_scores("feline hunting".split())
print(scores)   # [2.18, 0.0, 0.0]  — only doc 0 has any matching tokens

scores = bm25.get_scores("cat hunting rodent".split())
print(scores)   # [4.31, 0.0, 0.0]  — higher: 3 matching tokens

# BM25 completely misses semantic near-matches
scores = bm25.get_scores("feline animals".split())
print(scores)   # [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]  — no lexical overlap with any doc

Dense models map text to a compact fixed-length vector (128–4096 dimensions). All values are non-zero — the full semantic meaning is distributed across every dimension. Similarity is computed by cosine or dot product, which is fast enough to search millions of vectors in milliseconds.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

docs = [
    "cats are small carnivores that hunt rodents",
    "dogs are loyal companions and working animals",
    "Python is a high-level programming language",
]
query = "feline animals"

doc_vecs = model.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)
q_vec = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)

scores = (q_vec @ doc_vecs.T)[0]
for doc, score in zip(docs, scores):
    print(f"{score:.3f}  {doc}")
# 0.742  cats are small carnivores that hunt rodents
# 0.547  dogs are loyal companions and working animals  ← semantic match
# 0.157  Python is a high-level programming language
The dense model correctly identifies both the cat and dog documents as animal-related, even though "feline" appears in neither. The sparse BM25 scored them all at 0.

Some bi-encoders are symmetric: queries and documents use the same model with no special treatment. Others are asymmetric: queries and documents go through different processing — typically a prefix or separate encoder — because short queries and long passages occupy different regions of the embedding space.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

# Symmetric — same model, no prefix
sym_model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
doc_vecs = sym_model.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)
q_vec = sym_model.encode(["feline animals"], normalize_embeddings=True)

# Asymmetric with e5 — requires 'query: ' / 'passage: ' prefixes
e5 = SentenceTransformer("intfloat/multilingual-e5-large")
doc_vecs_e5 = e5.encode(
    [f"passage: {d}" for d in docs],
    normalize_embeddings=True,
)
q_vec_e5 = e5.encode(
    ["query: feline animals"],  # ← prefix required
    normalize_embeddings=True,
)

# Asymmetric with bge via prompt_name
bge = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")
doc_vecs_bge = bge.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)       # no prefix for docs
q_vec_bge = bge.encode(
    ["feline animals"],
    prompt_name="query",       # applies model-specific query prefix
    normalize_embeddings=True,
)

A cross-encoder takes a (query, document) pair as a single concatenated input and outputs a single relevance score. This joint encoding lets the model attend across both texts simultaneously, producing much more accurate relevance judgements than a bi-encoder — but at the cost of being impossible to pre-compute: you must run the model separately for every (query, document) pair at query time.

from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

reranker = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")

query = "feline animals"

# Cross-encoder takes (query, doc) pairs — no separate encode step
pairs = [(query, doc) for doc in docs]
scores = reranker.predict(pairs)   # shape (3,)
print(scores)
# e.g. [ 5.32, 2.18, -3.87]
# Higher = more relevant. Calibration varies by model — compare relative, not absolute.

# In practice: bi-encoder retrieves top-100, cross-encoder reranks them
# Cross-encoder is ~100x slower per-doc than bi-encoder, but only runs on 100 docs
ranked = sorted(zip(docs, scores), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
for doc, score in ranked:
    print(f"{score:.2f}  {doc}")
Every embedding model has a maximum token input length. Text beyond that limit is truncated silently — the tail of a long document is ignored.

For RAG pipelines, chunk documents to comfortably fit within the model's window (e.g. 512 tokens for a 512-limit model, leaving room for overlap). For models with 8k+ context, you can embed whole sections or short documents without chunking.

Search engines, recommendation systems, and RAG pipelines all fundamentally depend on the ability to measure semantic similarity between texts. Before neural embeddings, this was done with keyword overlap (TF-IDF, BM25) — effective for exact matches but blind to meaning. A query for 'cardiac arrest treatment' would miss documents about 'heart attack management' even though they are semantically identical.

Dense text embeddings changed this. By mapping text to a vector space where semantically similar texts are geometrically close, modern embedding models enable retrieval that works across vocabulary, writing style, and even languages. This is the foundation of every production RAG system: documents are embedded at index time, queries are embedded at search time, and the nearest neighbours in embedding space are the relevant passages.

The choice of embedding model directly controls the quality ceiling of everything downstream. A retrieval pipeline that surfaces the wrong passages will produce wrong answers regardless of how powerful the generation model is — 'garbage in, garbage out' at the retrieval layer. Understanding the three model families (sparse, dense bi-encoder, cross-encoder) is therefore prerequisite knowledge for building reliable LLM-powered applications.
Dense models capture meaning, not just word overlap. 'feline' and 'cat' embed near each other; BM25 gives them zero overlap.
Regardless of input length, the output is always a d-dimensional vector. This enables pre-computation and efficient vector database indexing.
With FAISS or similar libraries, you can find the nearest neighbour among 10 million vectors in under 10 milliseconds using approximate nearest neighbour (ANN) search.
Models like bge-m3 and multilingual-e5 embed across 100+ languages into the same space, enabling cross-lingual search (query in English, retrieve French documents).
The same embedding model works for semantic search, clustering, classification, deduplication, and recommendation — one model, many downstream tasks.

Most embedding models truncate input silently at their token limit (256 for MiniLM-L6, 8192 for bge-m3). Text beyond the limit is ignored — the entire tail of a long document may be missing from the embedding.
Dense models struggle with proper nouns, product codes, and technical jargon not seen during training. 'SKU-A7X-2024' has no meaningful embedding. Hybrid retrieval (BM25 + dense) handles this.

Bi-encoder models cannot adapt their representation to the specific query at retrieval time — the document embedding is computed once and reused. Cross-encoders can attend across query and document but cannot pre-compute.
A 768-dimension float32 embedding takes 3 KB. One million documents require 3 GB just for embeddings. Use INT8 quantisation (PQ in FAISS) to reduce to ~1 GB with acceptable accuracy loss.
When you switch embedding models, every document in the index must be re-embedded. At 100M documents this is expensive — plan model selection carefully.
When to Use Each Embedding Family
Queries are keyword-heavy: product codes, part numbers, names, technical identifiers.
You cannot afford the GPU memory for a dense embedding index.
Your domain is highly specialised and dense models haven't seen enough training data.
Latency budget is under 10ms and the corpus is under 100k documents.
Queries use natural language that may not match document keywords exactly.
You need to retrieve across languages or writing styles.
The task is semantic search, RAG, clustering, or recommendation.
You need to pre-compute embeddings once and serve at scale.
You need maximum precision on a shortlist (top-5 results for high-stakes use case).
Bi-encoder retrieval quality is insufficient after initial filtering.
You are building a two-stage pipeline: bi-encoder retrieves top-100, cross-encoder reranks to top-5.
The corpus is small enough that O(N) scoring at query time is affordable.
How to Implement a Complete RAG Retrieval Pipeline

A production retrieval pipeline combines BM25 and dense bi-encoder retrieval (hybrid search), then optionally reranks the results with a cross-encoder. This is called the retrieve-rerank pipeline and consistently outperforms any single retrieval method.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, CrossEncoder
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
import numpy as np
from typing import list


class HybridRetriever:
    """
    Two-stage retrieval: BM25 + dense bi-encoder fusion, then cross-encoder rerank.

    Stage 1: Retrieve top-k candidates using BM25 and bi-encoder independently.
    Stage 2: Fuse results with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF).
    Stage 3: Rerank top-m with a cross-encoder for maximum precision.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        bi_encoder_name: str = "BAAI/bge-m3",
        cross_encoder_name: str = "cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2",
    ) -> None:
        self.bi_encoder = SentenceTransformer(bi_encoder_name)
        self.cross_encoder = CrossEncoder(cross_encoder_name)
        self.docs: list[str] = []
        self.bm25: BM25Okapi | None = None
        self.doc_embeddings: np.ndarray | None = None

    def index(self, docs: list[str]) -> None:
        """Pre-compute BM25 index and dense embeddings."""
        self.docs = docs
        # BM25: simple whitespace tokenisation (use spaCy for better results)
        self.bm25 = BM25Okapi([d.lower().split() for d in docs])
        # Dense: embed with bi-encoder, normalise for cosine dot-product
        print(f"Embedding {len(docs)} documents...")
        self.doc_embeddings = self.bi_encoder.encode(
            docs, normalize_embeddings=True, show_progress_bar=True, batch_size=64
        )  # shape (N, d)

    def retrieve(self, query: str, top_k: int = 20) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
        """
        Fuse BM25 and dense retrieval with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF).
        RRF score(d, r) = sum(1 / (k + rank_r(d))) for each ranked list r.
        """        assert self.bm25 is not None, "Call .index() first"

        # BM25 ranking
        bm25_scores = self.bm25.get_scores(query.lower().split())
        bm25_ranked = np.argsort(bm25_scores)[::-1][:top_k]

        # Dense bi-encoder ranking
        q_emb = self.bi_encoder.encode(
            [query], normalize_embeddings=True, prompt_name="query"
        )
        dense_scores = (q_emb @ self.doc_embeddings.T)[0]
        dense_ranked = np.argsort(dense_scores)[::-1][:top_k]

        # RRF fusion
        rrf_k = 60  # constant that dampens the effect of rank position
        rrf = {}
        for rank, idx in enumerate(bm25_ranked):
            rrf[idx] = rrf.get(idx, 0.0) + 1.0 / (rrf_k + rank + 1)
        for rank, idx in enumerate(dense_ranked):
            rrf[idx] = rrf.get(idx, 0.0) + 1.0 / (rrf_k + rank + 1)

        candidates = sorted(rrf.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:top_k]
        return [(self.docs[idx], score) for idx, score in candidates]

    def retrieve_and_rerank(
        self, query: str, top_k: int = 20, final_k: int = 5
    ) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
        """Full pipeline: hybrid retrieval → cross-encoder reranking."""
        candidates = self.retrieve(query, top_k)
        pairs = [(query, doc) for doc, _ in candidates]
        rerank_scores = self.cross_encoder.predict(pairs)
        ranked = sorted(
            zip([doc for doc, _ in candidates], rerank_scores),
            key=lambda x: x[1],
            reverse=True,
        )
        return ranked[:final_k]


# Usage
docs = [
    "PostgreSQL uses MVCC for concurrency control in ACID transactions.",
    "Redis supports pub/sub messaging patterns for real-time applications.",
    "A B-tree index allows O(log n) point lookups in relational databases.",
    "Elasticsearch uses inverted indices for full-text search.",
    "Consistent hashing distributes data across nodes in distributed databases.",
]

retriever = HybridRetriever()
retriever.index(docs)

results = retriever.retrieve_and_rerank("How do databases handle concurrent reads?")
for doc, score in results:
    print(f"{score:6.3f}  {doc}")
Real-World Example: Building a Code Documentation Search

A developer platform embeds its entire API documentation (10,000 pages) and serves semantic search over it. The challenge: documentation uses technical jargon and code snippets that BM25 handles well, but user queries are often natural language descriptions of what they want to do. Hybrid retrieval solves this.

import anthropic
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import json, numpy as np

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Simulated documentation chunks (in production: chunked from actual docs)
DOC_CHUNKS = [
    {"id": "auth-1", "text": "client.messages.create() requires an API key set via ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var."},
    {"id": "stream-1", "text": "Use client.messages.stream() to receive Server-Sent Events incrementally."},
    {"id": "tool-1", "text": "Tool use: define tools in the tools= parameter as a list of dicts with name, description, input_schema."},
    {"id": "vision-1", "text": "Pass images as base64-encoded strings in the content array with type='image'."},
    {"id": "batch-1", "text": "Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously; use client.beta.messages.batches.create()."},
]

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

def build_doc_index(chunks: list[dict]) -> tuple[np.ndarray, list[dict]]:
    texts = [c["text"] for c in chunks]
    embeddings = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
    return embeddings, chunks

def semantic_doc_search(query: str, embeddings: np.ndarray, chunks: list[dict], k: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
    q_emb = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
    scores = (q_emb @ embeddings.T)[0]
    top_k = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:k]
    return [chunks[i] for i in top_k]

doc_embeddings, all_chunks = build_doc_index(DOC_CHUNKS)

def answer_with_rag(user_question: str) -> str:
    """Retrieve relevant docs, then answer with Claude."""
    retrieved = semantic_doc_search(user_question, doc_embeddings, all_chunks)
    context = "\n".join(f"- {c['text']}" for c in retrieved)

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=300,
        system="You are a technical assistant for the Anthropic API. Answer based only on the provided documentation.",
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Documentation:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {user_question}"
        }],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

question = "How do I send images to the API?"
print(f"Q: {question}")
print(f"A: {answer_with_rag(question)}")
e5-large and bge models require 'query: ' / 'passage: ' prefixes. Forgetting them produces embeddings from a different semantic space and dramatically reduces retrieval accuracy.
Hard boundaries between chunks split information mid-sentence. A question about a concept that straddles two chunks retrieves neither. Use 10–20% token overlap between consecutive chunks.
Using the same embedding for indexing and fine-tuning:

If you fine-tune the bi-encoder on new data, you must re-embed every document in the index. Skipping this step creates a mismatch: the query embedding and document embeddings are from different model versions.
Not normalising embeddings for dot-product search:
FAISS's IndexFlatIP computes dot products. If embeddings are not L2-normalised, dot product is not equal to cosine similarity. Always call normalize_embeddings=True or normalise manually.

all-MiniLM-L6-v2 truncates at 256 tokens. A 500-word document is embedded from only its first ~200 words. The second half is invisible to the retrieval system. Use a model with a larger context window for long documents.

# WRONG: missing asymmetric prefix for e5 model
e5 = SentenceTransformer("intfloat/multilingual-e5-large")
q_emb_wrong = e5.encode(["What is quantum entanglement?"], normalize_embeddings=True)
# This embeds the query as if it were a passage — wrong embedding space

# CORRECT
q_emb_right = e5.encode(["query: What is quantum entanglement?"], normalize_embeddings=True)
doc_emb = e5.encode(["passage: Quantum entanglement is a phenomenon..."], normalize_embeddings=True)

# WRONG: not normalising for dot-product FAISS index
import faiss
import numpy as np
raw_embs = np.random.randn(1000, 384).astype("float32")  # unnormalised
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(384)
index.add(raw_embs)  # dot product ≠ cosine similarity here

# CORRECT
norms = np.linalg.norm(raw_embs, axis=1, keepdims=True)
normalised = raw_embs / norms
index.add(normalised)  # now dot product == cosine similarity
Start with a general-purpose model and measure:
bge-m3 or nomic-embed-text-v1.5 are strong defaults. Fine-tune only after measuring retrieval quality (Recall@10) on your actual domain queries. Fine-tuning is expensive — data often matters more.
Use hybrid retrieval for production systems:
Combine BM25 and bi-encoder with Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Hybrid systems consistently outperform either method alone, especially on queries with rare keywords or exact identifiers.
Add a cross-encoder reranker for high-precision use cases:
Retrieve 50–100 candidates with the bi-encoder, rerank the top results with a cross-encoder. This recovers most of the accuracy loss from the bi-encoder's approximation.
Use 10–20% token overlap between consecutive chunks. For a 256-token chunk size, overlap by 25–50 tokens. This prevents information from being split across chunk boundaries.
Monitor retrieval quality separately from generation quality:
Track Recall@K (does the correct document appear in the top K results?) independently of generation metrics. A low Recall@K cannot be recovered by a better LLM.

Embedding throughput on CPU vs GPU varies by approximately 50–100x. For a corpus of 1 million documents, indexing time on CPU (MiniLM-L6) is roughly 2 hours; on a single A100 it is under 5 minutes. For ongoing updates (stream of new documents), embedding in real-time requires GPU or a dedicated embedding service.

For approximate nearest neighbour search (ANN) at scale, FAISS is the standard library. Use HNSW (IndexHNSWFlat) for the best recall-latency tradeoff in memory, or IVF-PQ (IndexIVFPQ) for datasets that don't fit in RAM.

import faiss
import numpy as np

d = 768   # embedding dimension
N = 1_000_000  # corpus size

# Build HNSW index (in-memory, good recall, fast search)
index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(d, 32)  # 32 = M parameter (graph connectivity)
index.hnsw.efConstruction = 200     # larger = better quality at build time
index.hnsw.efSearch = 50            # larger = better recall at search time

# Add embeddings (must be float32)
embeddings = np.random.randn(N, d).astype(np.float32)
faiss.normalize_L2(embeddings)
index.add(embeddings)

# Search: top-5 nearest neighbours
query = np.random.randn(1, d).astype(np.float32)
faiss.normalize_L2(query)
distances, indices = index.search(query, 5)
# distances: cosine similarities (dot product after normalisation)
# indices: row numbers in the embeddings array
Python library for bi-encoders and cross-encoders; supports 500+ models from HuggingFace Hub
CPU/GPU approximate nearest neighbour library; industry standard for large-scale retrieval
Managed vector databases with filtering, metadata storage, and auto-scaling
Pure-Python BM25 for small corpora (< 100k docs); easy to combine with dense retrieval
RAG frameworks that handle chunking, embedding, indexing, and retrieval pipeline orchestration
Massive Text Embedding Benchmark — standard leaderboard for comparing embedding models across 56 tasks

Embedding models introduce attack surfaces specific to retrieval systems. The most important is embedding inversion — given a dense embedding vector, it is sometimes possible to approximately reconstruct the original text. This means embeddings stored in a vector database are not fully opaque representations of sensitive documents.

Research has demonstrated that language model embeddings can be partially inverted to reconstruct source text. Do not store embeddings of highly sensitive text (PII, trade secrets) in multi-tenant vector databases without additional access controls.

In RAG systems, malicious content in the document corpus can inject instructions into the LLM's context when that document is retrieved. Apply output filtering and carefully scope the LLM's authority within the RAG pipeline.
An attacker with write access to the document store can embed crafted documents designed to rank highly for specific queries, causing the LLM to generate based on false information.

Always re-embed the entire corpus when upgrading the embedding model. Partial re-embedding (only new documents) creates a mixed index where old and new embeddings are from different spaces, degrading recall unpredictably.
Sparse (BM25) for exact keyword match, dense bi-encoder for semantic retrieval at scale, cross-encoder for maximum-precision reranking of short candidate lists.
BM25 + bi-encoder fused with Reciprocal Rank Fusion outperforms either method alone on heterogeneous real-world queries.
e5, bge, and similar models distinguish query from passage representations. Missing the prefix produces wrong embeddings and poor retrieval quality.
All embedding models have a token limit. Text beyond that limit is dropped without warning. Chunk documents to fit within the limit, with overlap at boundaries.
Cross-encoders are O(N) per query — only use them on shortlists:
The standard pattern is bi-encoder retrieves top-100, cross-encoder reranks to top-5. Never run a cross-encoder over a full corpus.
L2 normalisation makes dot product equal cosine similarity, enabling correct ranking in FAISS IndexFlatIP or any ANN index.
Track Recall@K as your primary retrieval metric:
Generation quality depends on retrieval quality. A generation model cannot compensate for the relevant document not appearing in the top K results.
